originalTitle: Trump news at a glance: president’s pool project plagued by peeling paint – and algae blooms
summary: Trump’s claims that ‘vandals’ are to blame don’t hold water so far – key US politics stories from Monday 22 June
content: Donald Trump is claiming – without providing evidence – that the sorry state of the Lincoln Memorial reflecting pool weeks after a $14m renovation is the work of “vandals”. On Monday, Trump was adamant it was not the pool company to blame for the algae blooms and peeling paint, instead pointing to five people arrested for vandalism and five more are under investigation. “It’s not a lot of damage, but we’ll probably have to let the water out and refix it. They went in there with a knife,” Trump told reporters Monday, describing what he first said was a 290- to 300ft slit in the paint but then later amended to a 350ft slit. He also said someone had put fertilizer into the water, which caused the algae to grow. When pushed to provide evidence of his claims, he told reporters to call the Department of the Interior and the National Park Service. Neither agency responded immediately to a request for comment, nor did the US park police. Trump has sought to turn the monument “American flag blue” in time for the for the country’s 250th birthday, which included painting the bottom of the pool a dark shade of navy officially called “Old Glory Blue”. He awarded a no-bid contract to a company he said had previously done work on swimming pools at one of his golf clubs, and within days of the completion of the work, the water started to appear green from algae plaguing the standing water and the coating of paint applied during the renovation also started to detach. Reflecting pool to be drained again as Trump claims five vandalism arrests It’s unclear when the pool will be drained, but a spokesperson with the DC Water Authority said the agency has issued the national parks service a temporary permit to discharge water into a sewer that flows into a local treatment facility. The permit was issued 16 June and expires 2 July, the spokesperson said. Read the full story US Senate passes bipartisan bill to lower housing costs The 21st Century Road to Housing Act would limit investors’ ability to buy homes, waives some federal permitting rules in a bid to ease new construction, and authorizes pilot programs to facilitate grants for home improvements and planning affordable housing. It passed the Senate overwhelmingly, with a vote of 85-5, and now heads to the House of Representatives. Read the full story Iran agrees to UN nuclear inspectors’ return as part of agreement with US Iran has agreed to allow UN nuclear inspectors back into the country as part of an agreement under which Washington will lift sanctions on Tehran’s oil exports and the strait of Hormuz will reopen, the US vice-president, JD Vance, has said. Read the full story Judge blocks subpoenas for Walz and others over Minnesota immigration crackdown A federal judge agreed to quash the US federal government’s subpoenas of leaders in Minnesota issued during the Trump administration’s controversial immigration crackdown on the state earlier this year. The US Department of Justice issued subpoenas to the Minnesota governor, Tim Walz; the attorney general, Keith Ellison; the Minneapolis mayor, Jacob Frey; and other local officials in the Twin Cities in January. Read the full story What else happened today: Gas ⁠station operators including BP, Circle K, Marathon, 7-Eleven, Walmart and Albertsons were sued on Monday by California drivers ⁠who accused them of using artificial intelligence to boost prices at the pump. Authorities in Illinois say that two older residents were killed and at least five other people were injured in a tornado that ripped through a rural county and destroyed several buildings on Sunday evening. The television network ABC is seeking the public’s backing as it faces simultaneous investigations from the Brendan Carr-led Federal Communications Commission (FCC). Alan Greenspan, the influential economist who steered US ⁠monetary policy ⁠during his ‌five ‌terms as chair ‌of the Federal Reserve ‌under four presidents, has died aged 100. The superintendent of Los Angeles public schools has resigned five months after the FBI served search warrants at his home and the LA Unified school district headquarters. Catching up? Here’s what happened on 21 June 2026.
chineseTitle: 特朗普新闻一览：总统的泳池项目受到油漆剥落和藻类繁殖的困扰
chineseSummary: 特朗普关于“破坏者”的说法迄今为止站不住脚——6 月 22 日星期一的美国重要政治新闻
chineseContent: 唐纳德·特朗普在没有提供证据的情况下声称，林肯纪念堂倒影池在花费 1400 万美元进行翻修几周后的惨状是“破坏者”造成的。周一，特朗​​普坚称藻类大量繁殖和油漆剥落不应归咎于泳池公司，而是指出五人因故意破坏公物而被捕，另外五人正在接受调查。特朗普周一对记者说：“损坏不是很大，但我们可能必须把水放掉并重新修复。他们拿着刀进去了。”他描述了他最初所说的油漆上有一个 290 到 300 英尺的裂缝，但后来修改为 350 英尺的裂缝。他还说有人在水里放了化肥，导致藻类生长。当被要求提供证据证明他的说法时，他告诉记者致电内政部和国家公园管理局。两家机构和美国公园警察都没有立即回应置评请求。特朗普试图在美国诞辰 250 周年之际将纪念碑变成“美国国旗蓝色”，其中包括将泳池底部涂成深海军色调，官方称为“旧荣耀蓝”。他向一家公司授予了一份无投标合同，他称该公司之前曾在他的一个高尔夫球俱乐部的游泳池进行过工程，工程完成后几天内，水开始因藻类困扰积水而呈现绿色，并且翻修期间涂的油漆涂层也开始脱落。特朗普声称逮捕了五名破坏公物的人，倒影池将再次排空 目前还不清楚倒影池何时会排空，但华盛顿水务局的一位发言人表示，该机构已向国家公园管理局颁发了临时许可证，可以将水排入下水道，然后流入当地的处理设施。许可证
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
